name: GSA Fundraising and Community Relations Committee
description:
GSA Fundraising and Community Relations Committee (FCR)

Mission of the FCR

The mission of the GSA Fundraising and Community Relations Committee (FCR) is to strategically plan and carry out fundraising initiatives and brand-building opportunities that strengthen the GSA and enhance its visibility within the University, alumni, and the broader community.

Leadership of the FCR

A. Chair and Vice-Chair

  • The FCR is chaired by the Executive Vice President (EVP).

  • A Vice-Chair must be elected by the committee.

B. Succession

  • If the EVP resigns, the GSA President will call the meeting, and the Vice-Chair will assume responsibility for chairing the committee to ensure continuity of business.

Fundraising Committee Policy

  • The FCR will follow all policies outlined in the FCR Handbook.

  • Any changes to the FCR Handbook must be approved by a vote of the Fundraising Committee and confirmed by the GSA Senior Advisor.

  • The Handbook will include procedures for:

    • Creating fundraising initiatives

    • Establishing timelines and strategies

    • Outlining responsibilities and procedures

FCR Strategy

A. Initiative Design
Each fundraising initiative must include:

  1. A clear fundraising purpose and target goals

  2. A strategic program plan

  3. An action plan

  4. An assessment plan

B. Scope of Responsibility

  • The FCR’s responsibility is limited to fundraising. Once funds are raised for a specific purpose (e.g., scholarships or awards), the distribution of those funds is handled by the relevant committee (such as the Awards Committee).

  • The FCR may not determine how other committees allocate funds once fundraising goals are met.

C. Collaboration

  • The GSA Council and other GSA Committees may submit fundraising requests to FCR to support growth and collaboration.

  • Council Members may also request fundraising support on behalf of their colleges.

D. Annual Planning
At the start of each academic year, the FCR will organize and develop strategies for both brand-building initiativesand fundraising initiatives.

Brand-Building Initiatives

  • The FCR will develop strategies for at least two brand-building events per academic year, partnering with alumni, community, and/or industry leaders.

  • Possible examples include the GSA Poster Symposium, Unity in Diversity, or the GSA Spring Awards.

  • The FCR must collaborate with the UNR Foundation Development Officers and, when necessary, other GSA Committees to plan these initiatives.

  • Final strategies must be discussed with the UNR Foundation Development Office before implementation.

GSA Fundraising Initiatives

  • The FCR will develop strategies, as outlined in the FCR Handbook, for at least two fundraising initiatives per academic year.

  • Examples may include: merchandise campaigns, food truck fundraisers, or a silent auction.

  • All fundraising strategies must be presented to and approved by the GSA Council.

Election of FCR Members

  • During the first meeting of the new GSA Council each academic year, the Council will elect four (4) representatives, including one (1) alternate, to serve on the FCR.
summary:
The GSA Fundraising and Community Relations Committee (FCR) is responsible
for strategically planning and executing fundraising initiatives and brand-building
opportunities for the GSA.

name: GSA Judicial, Budget & Finance Committee
description:
GSA Judicial, Budget, & Finance Committee (JB&F)

Mission of the JB&F

The mission of the Judicial, Budget, and Finance Committee (JB&F) is to:

  • Review and amend the GSA Constitution and Bylaws as needed.

  • Review and evaluate GSA policies to ensure compliance, clarity, and effectiveness.

Leadership of the JB&F

A. Chair and Vice-Chair

  • The JB&F is chaired by the Internal Vice President (IVP).

  • A Vice-Chair must be elected by the committee.

B. Succession

  • If the IVP resigns, the GSA President will call a JB&F meeting.

  • The Vice-Chair will assume responsibility for chairing the committee until further leadership is established.

Censure, Grievances, and Removal Procedures

  • The JB&F will oversee all grievance and removal procedures as outlined in Article III of the GSA Bylaws.

GSA Budget Process & Approval

A. Budget Creation

  1. The JB&F determines the annual GSA budget at the start of the academic year.

  2. Once final enrollment numbers are published, the JB&F prepares a proposed budget and works with the GSA Senior Advisor and Executive Council to finalize it.

  3. The proposed budget is presented to the GSA Council for approval at the first Council meeting of the academic year.

B. Budget Approval Process

  1. The Council may approve, amend, or reject the proposed budget.

  2. If rejected, the budget is returned to the JB&F for revision.

  3. If rejected twice, the GSA Executive Team, with the Senior Advisor present, will prepare a new budget proposal using the previous Fall’s approved budget as an interim budget.

  4. The process repeats until the Council approves a budget.

C. Reporting and Oversight

  • At the first Council meeting, the Senior Advisor must present the previous year’s financial report and the proposed budget for the new year.

  • Financial reports will also be provided upon request by the Executive Council or Council.

D. Funding Restrictions

  • Retroactive funding is prohibited.

  • Reimbursements of up to $50 may be approved by the Council for GSA Representatives, Executive Officers, or employees for expenses directly tied to GSA activities.

  • Reimbursement requests must be submitted within 30 days of purchase.

  • This rule may be waived if the Senior Advisor verifies that proper funding request procedures were followed.

  • Individual students may not be funded for academic pursuits outside of official GSA programs.

JB&F Liaison Positions

A. Role of Liaisons

  • JB&F members will serve as liaisons to other GSA Committees.

  • Liaisons ensure committees follow:

    • GSA Bylaws

    • Nevada Open Meeting Laws

    • Robert’s Rules of Order

  • Liaisons also ensure committees remain within their approved budgets.

B. Conflict of Interest

  • A JB&F member serving on a committee cannot act as the liaison to that committee.

C. Reporting and Enforcement

  1. If violations occur (e.g., failing to post agendas/minutes, not meeting quorum, walking quorums), the liaison must report them to JB&F.

  2. JB&F will notify the committee chair, IVP, and Senior Advisor in writing to begin an investigation.

  3. The committee has five (5) business days to respond in writing with an explanation.

  4. JB&F may then:

    • Allow remedial actions, or

    • Recommend removal of the chair or committee member(s).

D. Escalation

  • If a second violation occurs, JB&F may escalate by requiring further evidence, beginning removal procedures, or recommending disciplinary action.

  • Removal of a Committee Chair requires:

    • A two-thirds (⅔) vote of JB&F, and

    • Approval from the Senior Advisor.

  • JB&F may also recommend that the IVP remove a committee member.

  • In cases of substantive violations, JB&F may begin removal procedures as defined in Article III of the Bylaws.

Election of JB&F Members

  • At the first meeting of the new GSA Council, the Council will elect four (4) representatives, including two (2) alternates, to serve on the JB&F.

  • A Vice-Chair must be elected within the committee.

  • The Vice-Chair may lead meetings in the absence of the Chair.
summary: The Mission of the GSA Judicial, Budget, and Finance Committee (JB&F) is to review and amend the Constitution and Bylaws when needed. GSA policies are also reviewed by JB&F.

name: Community of English Spanish Speakers
description:
There is an increasing number of Spanish-speaking patients in hospitals across the nation, so why aren't there more Spanish-English bilingual providers and researchers?  In the Northern Nevada region (Washoe County), approximately 25% of residents are bilingual. Importantly, access to a bilingual translator in hospitals and clinics is very limited given the dearth in bilingual hospitalists, nurses, and specialized physicians. In addition, bilingual competency in the STEM fields and in the medical curriculum is low which underscores the need to increase the number of bilingual, practicing physicians in urban and rural communities. The Community of Bilingual English-Spanish Speakers (CBESS), a pipeline program to increase linguistic and geographic diversity in STEM + Health, seeks to increase the number of bilingual English-Spanish students who pursue paths in science, technology, engineering, math (STEM)-healthcare fields such as biomedical, behavioral and clinical careers. 

The Latino Research Center and School of Medicine, in collaboration with the Raggio Research Center, will create opportunities to position bilingual K-12 high school students as insiders into STEM-healthcare fields.  In other words, these students will be well-equipped with the knowledge, skills and attitudes to participate in continuing their education in a clinical or research workforce, and thereby, become well-rounded individuals.
summary: A pipeline program to increase linguistic and geographic diversity in STEM and Healthcare that seeks to increase the number of bilingual English-Spanish students who pursue paths in science, technology, engineering, math (STEM)-healthcare fields.

name: GSA Clubs & Organizations Committee
description:
GSA Clubs & Organizations Committee (C&OC)

Mission of the GSA Clubs & Organizations Committee (C&OC)

The GSA Clubs and Organizations Committee (C&OC) supports graduate student engagement by:

  • Reviewing funding requests, reimbursement requests, and recognition applications from GSA-recognized clubs and organizations.

  • Promoting a strong, vibrant graduate student community through fair policies and opportunities for involvement.

Responsibilities of the GSA Clubs & Organizations Committee (C&OC)

A. Policy and Handbook
The C&OC is responsible for creating and updating policies for recognizing graduate student clubs and organizations. These policies will be outlined in the C&OC Handbook.

B. Accessibility of the Handbook
The C&OC Handbook will be made available to all graduate students for transparency and easy reference.

C. Annual Club Fair
The C&OC will organize a Club Fair once per academic year.

  • A successful Club Fair will include participation from multiple recognized GSA clubs and organizations.

  • The event should engage graduate students and encourage them to join, lead, and collaborate through campus clubs.

Recognition, Funding, and Regulations

A. Recognition Requirements
To receive official GSA recognition, clubs and organizations must follow the policies outlined in the C&OC Handbook, including:

  1. Attending at least one (1) C&OC meeting per academic year, with an executive member present.

  2. Submitting an updated membership roster annually, as required by the C&OC Handbook.

B. Funding Guidelines

  1. Each recognized club may apply for up to $700 per year.

  2. The amount awarded will depend on:

    • The overall GSA budget,

    • Club membership size,

    • The reach and impact of the club’s activities, and

    • The total number of active clubs.
      Note: An executive member must attend a C&OC meeting to request and receive funding approval.

  3. Items not funded by the GSA include (but are not limited to): honorariums, gifts, alcohol, and prizes.

  4. The GSA does not support or fund alcohol purchases.

    • If a club independently provides alcohol at an event, it is that club’s responsibility to check IDs and ensure alcohol is not served to anyone under 21.
summary: The GSA Clubs and Organizations Committee (C&OC) is responsible for reviewing funding, reimbursement, and recognition requests from clubs that have received GSA recognition

name: GSA Service Committee
description:
GSA Service Committee

Mission of the GSA Service Committee

The mission of the GSA Service Committee is to promote civic engagement and provide meaningful service opportunities for:

  • The graduate student body

  • The University community

  • The greater Reno community

Responsibilities of the GSA Service Committee

A. Donation Drive

  1. The committee is responsible for planning and managing donation drives each semester. Tasks include, but are not limited to:

    • Setting dates and reserving rooms

    • Creating and distributing advertisements

    • Setting up and monitoring donation bins

    • Promoting the drive to students, faculty, staff, and alumni

    • Organizing the donation shop and volunteering during the event

    • Breaking down the drive afterward

    • Voting on where leftover donations should go

    • Delivering donations to the chosen location

  2. A donation drive will be held once per semester.

  3. At least one drive per academic year must be a Clothing Drive.

B. GSA Day of Service

  1. The committee will organize a GSA Day of Service once per semester.

  2. Responsibilities include:

    • Selecting and planning a service project

    • Creating and distributing advertisements

    • Executing and supporting the event on the day

name: GSA Student Advocacy Committee
description:
GSA Advocacy Committee

Responsibilities of the GSA Advocacy Committee

  • Represent Graduate Student Interests

    • Identify and prioritize issues affecting graduate students (e.g., funding, workload, fees, health insurance, campus resources).

    • Serve as the voice of graduate students in discussions with university administration, faculty, and governing bodies.

  • Policy & Institutional Engagement

    • Monitor university policies, procedures, and initiatives that impact graduate students.

    • Draft recommendations, resolutions, or position statements on behalf of the GSA.

    • Participate in meetings with campus leadership to advocate for graduate student needs.

  • Awareness & Education

    • Inform graduate students about their rights, resources, and opportunities.

    • Host workshops, forums, or info sessions on advocacy-related issues (e.g., student rights, funding opportunities, health benefits).

  • Organize Advocacy Campaigns

    • Plan and implement initiatives to address systemic or emerging graduate student concerns.

    • Coordinate petitions, surveys, and outreach efforts to gather student input and build support.

  • Collaboration

    • Work with other GSA committees, student groups, and external organizations to strengthen advocacy efforts.

    • Build coalitions with faculty and staff allies to support student-centered policies.

  • Communication & Transparency

    • Provide regular updates to the GSA Council and graduate student body on advocacy work and outcomes.

    • Ensure graduate student concerns are heard and responded to in a timely manner.

  • Evaluation & Accountability

    • Assess the effectiveness of advocacy actions and recommend adjustments.

    • Document and report committee activities to ensure continuity for future members.
summary:
The mission of the Student Advocacy Committee is to advocate for the rights and
needs of graduate students and act as a liaison between the students and upper
administration to resolve student issues.

See our section on graduate resources!

name: GSA Awards Committee
description:
GSA Awards Committee

Responsibilities of the GSA Awards Committee

A. GSA Poster Symposium and Spring Awards
The Awards Committee helps make these two major events successful every year. Their work includes:

  • Checking and updating the rules and criteria for each award.
  • Asking faculty members to serve as judges.
  • Creating clear rubrics so judging is fair and consistent.
  • Planning and managing the budget for each award.
  • Organizing, promoting, and running both the GSA Poster Symposium and the GSA Spring Awards.

Note: To keep judging fair, the GSA Senior Advisor will oversee the judging process for both events.

B. Member of the Semester Award
At the beginning of the Fall semester, the Awards Committee will review the rules for the GSA Member of the Semester Award and share them with the GSA Council. This award will be given at the end of each semester to recognize members who make outstanding contributions to GSA.

C. New Award Proposals
The committee will also review any ideas for new GSA awards to keep recognition opportunities fresh and meaningful.

Eligibility to Participate in the GSA Spring Awards

A. Committee Members
Members of the GSA Awards Committee can apply for the GSA Spring Awards.

B. Executive Members
GSA Executive members cannot apply for the GSA Spring Awards to ensure fairness.

 

Want to know our agendas/minutes? Click this link.
summary:
The GSA Awards Committee is responsible for organizing the GSA Fall Poster
Symposium, GSA Spring Awards, and The Allison Ferguson Leadership Award.

name: GSA Events Committee
description:
GSA Events Committee

Mission of the GSA Events Committee

A. Purpose
The mission of the GSA Events Committee is to create opportunities for graduate students to come together, connect, and build community. Signature events include, but are not limited to:

  • The Welcome Back Social

  • The Fall and Spring Socials

  • Graduate Appreciation Week

B. Committee Responsibilities
Tasks of the Events Committee include, but are not limited to:

  • Selecting dates and timelines for events.

  • Reserving event locations.

  • Promoting events through flyers, emails, and social media.

  • Supporting event set-up, coordination, and logistics.

Responsibilities of the GSA Events Committee

A. Welcome Back Social

  1. This event will be held at the start of each academic year to welcome graduate students back to campus.

  2. The event should provide opportunities for networking, building friendships, and fostering a sense of community.

B. Fall and Spring Socials

  1. At least one social must be held each semester (Fall and Spring).

  2. The committee should plan these events with the goal of reaching as many graduate students as possible.

  3. If only two socials are held during the year, it is recommended that at least one of them be family-friendly.

C. Graduate Appreciation Week

  1. The committee will design and approve a timeline of activities and events for Graduate Appreciation Week.

  2. The proposed schedule must be presented to the GSA Council at least six (6) weeks before the event.
summary:
The mission of the GSA Events Committee is to coordinate events where Graduate
Students are able to come together and socialize, including but not limited to the
Welcome Back Social, the Fall and Spring Socials, and Graduate Appreciation
Week.

name: UNR Bhangra Giddha
description:
The Bhangra Giddha Club is a dynamic cultural organization dedicated to preserving and celebrating the vibrant traditions of Punjabi dance and music. With a focus on the energetic rhythms of Bhangra and the graceful, storytelling moves of Giddha, the club serves as a platform for individuals to connect with their cultural roots, express themselves creatively, and build a sense of community.

 

Whether you’re an experienced dancer or someone eager to learn, the club welcomes members from all backgrounds who share a passion for Punjabi culture. Through regular practice sessions, members master traditional and modern choreography, blending the timeless spirit of Bhangra and Giddha with contemporary styles. The club actively participates in cultural festivals, campus events, and competitions, showcasing the rich heritage of Punjab and spreading its infectious energy to diverse audiences.

 

More than just a dance group, the Bhangra Giddha Club fosters a supportive environment where members build friendships, celebrate diversity, and develop confidence through performance. It’s a place to learn, grow, and share the joy of Punjabi traditions while promoting inclusivity and cultural awareness. Join us to experience the rhythm, energy, and pride of Bhangra and Giddha!
summary: The Bhangra Giddha Club celebrates Punjabi culture through energetic Bhangra and graceful Giddha dance. Open to all, it brings people together to connect, learn, and perform, spreading joy and tradition.

name: The Nevada Medical Research Consortium
description:
The NMRC works with the UNR Associate Vice President of Research, Dr. Josh Baker, to connect undergraduates with medical research opportunities and longitudinal mentorship. The consortium aims to help prospective researchers gain residency in laboratories; map careers; apply for grants (NURA, PREP, etc.), fellowships (MARC, etc.), and scholarships (GRFP, NDSEG, etc.) in the biomedical sciences; apply for graduate and medical school; publishing; presenting at conferences; and answers questions and provides guidance on novel research obstacles. This stands apart from the UNR Undergraduate Research (UR) Office by broadening the scope of mentorship (UR does not assist with out-of-organization applications like MARC, GRFP, etc.), providing longitudinal mentorship for students (UR does not assist with laboratory residency or sponsorship, out-of-organization publishing, career mapping, etc.), and providing targeted approaches to specifically medial research on campus. Additionally, NMRC will foster collaboration between laboratories for joint research projects, provide networking opportunities for students, and engage in critical discourse surrounding relevant scientific advancements through the implementation of department-specific journal clubs. Journal clubs are composed of individuals from a specific scientific field who meet periodically to evaluate novel scientific discoveries and advancements. Medical and scientific literature will be reviewed to create informed medical scientists familiar with the most recent discoveries in their field.

We currently are comprised of an executive board and 6 departments (biochemistry, chemistry and pharmacology, clinical/population health, microbiology and immunology, neuroscience, and psychology and sociology) lead by top student researchers at UNR.
summary: Our mission is to generate longitudinal mentorship for researchers, foster interdisciplinary collaboration between laboratories, and create informed medical scientists for Nevada.

SHAPE is a student-led organization dedicated to empowering and mentoring undergraduate freshmen and sophomores who are interested in pre-professional fields. Our goal is to guide members through the early stages of their academic and career journeys by fostering leadership skills, providing meaningful networking opportunities, and offering personalized support tailored to each student’s unique goals.

We understand that the transition into college and the pursuit of professional ambitions can be overwhelming. That’s why SHAPE focuses on creating a supportive community where underclassmen can connect with peers, upperclassmen, and professionals who have navigated similar paths. Through workshops, mentorship programs, and networking events, we equip members with the knowledge, skills, and confidence needed to succeed academically and professionally.

Our commitment extends beyond just career preparation — we are dedicated to nurturing personal growth, resilience, and leadership development. SHAPE members learn how to set goals, build strong professional relationships, and access valuable resources, empowering them to take charge of their futures.

Whether you’re exploring career options, seeking guidance on internships and research opportunities, or aiming to develop essential soft skills, SHAPE is here to support and inspire you every step of the way.

Join SHAPE to build your network, sharpen your ambitions, and excel in your path toward professional excellence.
summary: We empower and mentor undergraduates in pre-professional fields by fostering leadership, building valuable networks, and offering personalized guidance—helping members grow personally and professionally to excel in their future careers.

Bridging Wellness is a student organization focused on fostering emotional well-being, connection, and open conversation within the Wolf Pack community. Each week, members meet to discuss topics related to mental health, student life, and personal growth through guided discussions and peer-led presentations. The club also maintains an anonymous submission system where students can share experiences or questions to be discussed safely and respectfully in meetings.

Beyond weekly discussions, Bridging Wellness engages in outreach that connects students to campus resources and supports broader awareness of student mental health needs. The club provides a space for students to be heard, learn from one another, and build a sense of belonging on campus.

BSA aims to create a welcoming environment for Bangladeshi students and anyone interested in learning about Bangladeshi culture, language, and history.

Through social gatherings, cultural nights, educational workshops, and community outreach, BSA serves as a platform for students to share their culture and build meaningful connections across campus. The organization also supports new and incoming Bangladeshi students by helping them adapt to university life and find a sense of belonging in the Wolf Pack community.

BSA’s activities include showcasing traditional music, dance, and cuisine; celebrating national and cultural holidays such as International Mother Language Day and Victory Day; and collaborating with other multicultural organizations to encourage diversity and cross-cultural understanding.

Open to all UNR students, BSA provides an inclusive space for creativity, leadership, and cultural exchange. Our goal is to foster unity among students while proudly representing Bangladesh’s rich cultural identity on campus.
summary: The Bangladesh Student Association promotes Bangladeshi culture, unity, and community at UNR through cultural events, social gatherings, and support for students interested in learning about Bangladesh.

summary: Human-centered AI education club aimed at inclusive development of AI literacy for UNR students of all backgrounds. We aim to demystify AI, invoke critical thinking and innovation, and embrace the uncertainty of a rapidly advancing technological world.

name: Pack Pilots
summary: Pack Pilots brings together students who dream of flight—whether as pilots, engineers, or enthusiasts—to share experiences, explore aviation, and build lifelong connections grounded in Wolf Pack pride.

name: Meals with Meaning
description:
Meals with Meaning is a student-led initiative dedicated to addressing local food insecurity through direct action and community collaboration. We provide a platform for students to engage in meaningful service by preparing meals for those experiencing food insecurity along with educating students and the community about food insecurity. 

Rather than functioning as a top-down service group, Meals with Meaning creates a flexible, supportive space where members can launch their own small-scale food outreach projects, collaborate with peers, and share ideas, recipes, safety tips, and resources. The club encourages service with the support of a like-minded community.

Our club empowers students to make a tangible difference while gaining experience in leadership, civic engagement, and community health. Through consistent, compassionate action, even on a small scale, we aim to foster meaningful connections and have a lasting impact in the community.
summary: Meals with Meaning is a student-led club where members prepare and distribute meals to people experiencing food insecurity, supporting projects while sharing ideas and resources to educate and make a positive community impact.

name: Distributive Education Clubs of America
description: Collegiate DECA is an international organization that prepares college students for careers in marketing, finance, hospitality, and management through real-world experiences, competitions, and leadership development. It offers opportunities to compete in events like case studies, participate in corporate challenges, receive career insights from professionals, and access exclusive scholarships and internships. The program aims to develop students into academically prepared, community-oriented, and professionally responsible leaders.
summary: DECA is an international organization for college students that prepares emerging leaders and entrepreneurs for careers in marketing, finance, hospitality, and management. It provides hands-on, real-world experiences, competitive events, and leadership.

name: Hippocratic Society at the University of Nevada, Reno
description:
Medicine is often the context for some of the most important experiences in human life. While much of medicine is rightly devoted to ways that science and technology can improve life and reduce suffering, the human meaning of these experiences that reach from birth to death is best captured in the languages of the humanities and the arts. 

However, despite their immense value, most pre-health students receive little training in understanding and appreciating the humanities. By stimulating thoughtful discussion on medical-themed stories and literature, we seek to foster empathy and critical consciousness among future healthcare professionals. This club is for anyone interested in connecting to the humanity at the heart of medicine.
summary: We are an organization that seeks to cultivate empathy and humanism among future medical professionals by engaging pre-health students in meaningful dialogue regarding literature, art, and the humanities.

name: Students Against ICE Abuses
summary: Students aiming to raise awareness of Immigration Customs and Enforcement misconduct and mismanagement, specifically on K-12 school grounds.

name: Nevada Women’s Flag Football Club
description: The University of Nevada, Reno Women’s Flag Football Club is a student-run athletic organization dedicated to promoting women’s participation in competitive flag football. Our mission is to create an empowering, inclusive environment where members can build leadership skills, teamwork, and school spirit through sport. We aim to represent the Wolf Pack with pride, athletic growth, community involvement, and lifelong friendships. All experience levels are welcome!
summary: The Nevada Women’s Flag Football Club is a competitive student organization dedicated to advancing women’s athletics and promoting the growth of flag football at the University of Nevada, Reno.

name: UNR Comics Club
description: UNR Comics Club is a space for anyone to come talk about comics, manga, graphic novels, creating comics and much more! We hold weekly meetings and regular events. Come join our Discord to talk with more like minded people and follow us on Instagram!
summary: UNR Comics Club is a space for anyone to come talk about comics, manga, graphic novels, creating comics and much more! We hold weekly meetings and regular events. Come join our Discord to talk with more like minded people and follow us on Instagram!

name: Nevada Origami
description:
Origami is an art that has existed almost as long as paper itself. Generally, it is accepted to be the practice of folding an uncut square of paper into completed models inspired by the world around us, though today there exists many variations of this ancient practice, including dollar bill origami, tessellations, and kusudama/modulars. Furthermore, its unique properties have allowed for applications in math, architecture, and engineering.

No matter your experience level, we provide an inclusive and encouraging environment to share your work, learn to fold new models, and generally share the joys of origami. We meet two times a month, where one of those days is used to teach a model, and the other is an open community folding session. If you are new, don’t worry about bringing any supplies, we will provide paper and anything else you might need to get started. Happy folding!
summary: We are an inclusive student organization dedicated to sharing the joys of origami with beginners and seasoned folders alike.

name: UNR Sikh Student Association
description:
The UNR Sikh Student Association (SSA) is a faith-based coalition dedicated to fostering community, inclusivity, and awareness of Sikh values and culture at the University of Nevada, Reno. The organization aims to create a welcoming space for Sikh students and anyone interested in learning about Sikhism. Through educational events, discussions, and community service projects, the SSA promotes the Sikh principles of equality, compassion, and selfless service (seva).

In addition to celebrating Sikh traditions and heritage, the association encourages interfaith dialogue and collaboration, helping students of all backgrounds connect through shared values of respect and unity. The UNR Sikh Student Association strives to strengthen cultural understanding and contribute positively to the campus and local community.
summary: The UNR Sikh Student Association is a faith-based coalition that fosters community, inclusivity, and awareness of Sikh values. We promote equality, compassion, and selfless service through cultural events, dialogue, and service.

name: University of Nevada Thousand Pound Club
description: To foster student-athletes committed to excellence in Weightlifting and Powerlifting by providing structured training, knowledgeable instruction, and a culture of progress, accountability, perseverance, and mental fortitude.
summary: To foster student-athletes committed to excellence in Weightlifting and Powerlifting by providing structured training, knowledgeable instruction, and a culture of progress, accountability, perseverance, and mental fortitude.

name: ASUN Department of Sustainability
summary: The ASUN Department of Sustainability is committed to pursuing sustainable initiatives both at UNR and in the Reno community.

name: University of Nevada, Reno Country Dance Club
description: The University of Nevada, Reno Country Dance Club is dedicated to creating a welcoming and inclusive community where students can learn, practice, and enjoy country swing and line dance. Our mission is to promote physical activity, confidence, and connection through dance while celebrating country dance culture in a fun, respectful, and supportive environment for dancers of all experience levels.
summary: The UNR Country Dance Club is dedicated to creating a welcoming and inclusive community where students can learn, practice, and enjoy country swing and line dance, promoting physical activity, confidence, and connection through country dance.

name: Women In Aviation
summary: The mission of the Women in Aviation Club to promote the advancement of women in all aviation and aerospace career fields and interests, provide networking and mentorship opportunities, support professional development, and foster a welcoming community

name: Reproductive Freedom for All UNR
description:
Reproductive Freedom For All at the University of Nevada, Reno (RFFA UNR) is a student-led organization that is dedicated to advocating for reproductive freedom through education, advocacy, and community engagement. RFFA works to create an inclusive and supportive space where students can learn about bodily autonomy, reproductive healthcare access, and the systems that impact marginalized communities.

RFFA UNR empowers students to take part in meaningful civic engagement through educational events, discussions, tabling, and volunteer opportunities. Our mission centers on providing accurate information, accessible resources, and opportunities for students to engage in advocacy that supports reproductive justice for all people.
summary: RFFA at UNR is a student led club that is focused in education and advocacy, centered on community engagement aiming for reproductive freedom for all. The club is an inclusive space for learning and discussions, as well as civic action.

name: American Red Cross
description: The American Red Cross at the University of Nevada, Reno is a student-run organization operating under the guidance of the American Red Cross Northern Nevada Region. The club mobilizes student volunteers to make a real impact through mission-related service projects. Members support preparedness education, blood drives, disaster response efforts, fundraising, and humanitarian outreach while upholding the values of the global Red Cross network.
summary: The American Red Cross at UNR engages students in humanitarian service through preparedness education, blood drives, disaster response support, fundraising, and community outreach.

name: The Packtivists
summary: The Packtivist student club provides an opportunity for individuals to engage with a community of students to end power-based violence and create a safe space for survivors.

name: UNR AIAA
description: With nearly 30,000 individual members from 91 countries, and 95 corporate members, AIAA is the world’s largest technical society dedicated to the global aerospace profession. Created in 1963 by the merger of the two great aerospace societies of the day, the American Rocket Society (founded in 1930 as the American Interplanetary Society), and the Institute of the Aerospace Sciences (established in 1933 as the Institute of the Aeronautical Sciences), AIAA carries forth a proud tradition of more than 80 years of aerospace leadership.
summary: UNR Student Chapter for the American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics

name: UNR Wargaming Club
summary: The UNR Wargaming Club is dedicated to building a community around tabletop strategy gaming, with a focus on Games Workshop titles such as Warhammer 40k and Age of Sigmar. Members engage in both painting and gameplay, fostering creativity, strategy.

name: The Outdoor Adventure and Leadership Club
description:
The Outdoor Adventure & Leadership (ODAL) Club brings together students who share a passion for exploring the outdoors and developing leadership through real-world, hands-on experiences. Rooted in the belief that adventure fosters personal growth and connection, the ODAL Club creates opportunities for students of all experience levels to learn new skills, challenge themselves, and build lasting friendships in Nevada’s incredible natural landscapes.

Members participate in a wide range of activities, including hiking, rafting, climbing, camping, backpacking, and snow sports. Each trip is designed to promote teamwork, communication, problem-solving, and outdoor safety, helping students grow as confident and capable leaders. The club also emphasizes environmental stewardship and sustainability, encouraging members to take an active role in preserving and appreciating Nevada’s wild places and the greater Sierra region.

Beyond outdoor trips, ODAL Club hosts workshops, leadership trainings, and community events that deepen students’ connection to the outdoors and to each other. The club actively supports awareness, development, and funding for UNR’s Outdoor Adventure Leadership minor—helping expand opportunities for future students to learn through experience, adventure, and exploration.

Whether you’re brand new to outdoor activities or an experienced adventurer, ODAL Club welcomes all students who are excited to learn, lead, and explore together.
summary: ODAL Club unites students through outdoor adventures like hiking, rafting, and climbing. We build leadership, teamwork, and environmental stewardship while supporting awareness, growth, and funding of UNR’s Outdoor Adventure Leadership minor.

name: Wolf Pack Trumpet Club
description: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1WEuCyY-60hYI0qtSEfVvs5hhrh9P5feqCi_YlY0APcI/edit?usp=sharing
summary: The Wolf Pack Trumpet Club is a student led organization dedicated to building strong campus and community connections through trumpet playing.

name: Animal Shelter Volunteering Club
description:
Our organization is dedicated to advocating for the well being of animals in our community by supporting local shelters with the care and resources they need. Through compassionate volunteer efforts, we work to ensure that every animal receives the love, respect, and attention they deserve.

We believe all animals should be treated with kindness and dignity. By partnering with shelters, organizing volunteer initiatives, and encouraging community involvement, we strive to create a safer and healthier environment for vulnerable animals. Together, we aim to make a meaningful and lasting difference, one life at a time.
summary: We advocate for the well being of animals in our community by supporting local shelters through volunteer efforts that ensure every animal receives love, respect, and proper care.

name: Wolf Pack Politics and Policy Club
description: Wolf Pack Politics and Policy Club is a non-partisan student organization for students interested in politics and policy at the local, state, national, and international levels. The organization seeks to expand students’ knowledge and engagement with politics and policy topics, help to build their professional networks for career development, and promote intellectual discourse on political topics on our campus for a more informed student body. The Wolf Pack Politics and Policy Club works closely with the Department of Political Science, but is open to all students.
summary: The Wolf Pack Politics and Policy Club is a non-partisan student organization for students interested in politics and policy. The organization seeks to expand students’ knowledge and engagement at the local, state, national, and international levels.

name: Visibility n positivity
description:
Welcome to Visibility n Positivity!

 

Motto: Come as you are.

 

Visibility n Positivity is a welcoming, gentle community created especially for students who want to be seen, supported, and celebrated just as they are. Here, you can connect, reflect, grow, and care for yourself and others in a space free from pressure, ranking, or judgment.

We believe life moves in seasons — sometimes we grow, sometimes we rest, and sometimes we show vulnerability. All of these are important and honored here.

Together, we also give back to local and national organizations, spreading kindness beyond our community.

At Visibility n Positivity, you are valued for exactly who you are.
Join us to be part of a supportive, caring community where you truly belong.

 

We're not active via PackLife, please refer to our social media handle @visibilitynpositivityreno
summary: Visibility n Positivity’s goal is to promote mental health resources and chronic illness/ autoimmune support. We will host workshops based around wellness and positivity and volunteer to the Reno community.

name: Rotaract
description: Rotaract is a global service and leadership organization sponsored by Rotary International for college students and young professionals. Rotaract clubs focus on community service, professional development, and international connection, providing members with opportunities to lead projects, develop leadership skills, and make a positive impact locally and globally.
summary: Rotaract is a global service and leadership organization sponsored by Rotary International for college students and young professionals. Rotaract clubs focus on community service, professional development, and international connection.

name: UNR Wellness Society
description:
As part of a national wellness community with over 70 college and city chapters across the United States, our chapter is dedicated to creating a campus culture that values health, balance, and overall well-being. We provide a supportive and inclusive space where students can prioritize their physical, mental, and emotional wellness while building meaningful connections with others who share similar values. Through events, discussions, and shared resources, we encourage mindful living, personal growth, and a balanced lifestyle that supports both academic success and life beyond college. Whether you are just beginning your wellness journey or looking to deepen your practices, The Wellness Society at UNR is here to empower you and help you thrive.

Join the current GroupMe to stay updated with info: https://groupme.com/join_group/113514172/EgIric40
summary: The Wellness Society at UNR is a student community centered on balance, movement, and intentional living. Through meetings as well as on and off campus fitness classes, we explore wellness as a practice that supports body, mind, and daily life.

name: Needle and Thread
description:
The mission of Needle and Thread is to foster a welcoming environment where skills can be shared throughout the community. We aspire to help people learn various fiber arts, teaching helpful life skills and promoting sustainability through upcycling and creation rather than consumption. Students can express themselves creatively, and have access to materials they need. 

 

Club meetings will consist of short lessons and introductions of a specific fiber art, ideas and instructions, and a short presentation of examples of said art. Members can use club materials for current group projects or personal projects during club meetings. Members can ask for help with instruction, tutorial, and tools specifically from club officers.

 

Tutorials and slides presented

https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1aqGLmwNSYn57CZUlyVtLmVL7uPpOE68XrDAjdZrha8I/edit?usp=drivesdk
summary: Meetings are short lessons and introductions of a specific fiber art, ideas and instructions, and a short presentation of examples of said art. Members can use club materials for current group projects or personal projects during club meetings.

name: Tiny Tots Health Initiative
description: Founded in 2019, Tiny Tots Health Initiative (formerly Teeth4Tots) is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit dedicated to improving children’s health literacy and empowering families with the tools and knowledge to lead healthier lives. Through creative, engaging programs and partnerships with clinics, schools, and communities, we make health education accessible, fun, and impactful.
summary: Tiny Tots Health Initiative is a health-based volunteering club focused on helping our pediatric community. Through creative, engaging programs and partnerships with clinics, and our community, we make health education accessible, fun, and impactful.

name: UNR Junk Journal Club
description: The UNR Junk Journal Club is a club that promotes mental health awareness by allowing students to journal and destress. Students are able to journal goals, set intentions, craft, or release academic stress.
summary: The UNR Junk Journal Club is a club that promotes mental health awareness by allowing students to journal and destress. Students are able to journal goals, set intentions, craft, or release academic stress.
